headline: Move better.
Get stronger.
Get back on your feet.
hero.lead: 22 Strong Foundation helps veterans and low-income youth access hands-on recovery care when insurance, cost, or other barriers get in the way.

mission.heading: Specialized recovery care for people whose next appointment is at risk.
mission.lead: A veteran or young athlete can have a real injury, a referral, and a care plan, and still lose progress when the next appointment isn't covered, delayed, or out of reach. That's the gap 22 Strong exists to close.
needParagraphs: Referrals arrive at the same pressure point: progress has started, but cost, coverage limits, timing, or the lack of the right connection puts the next visit at risk.

22 Strong verifies the access gap and pays for recovery sessions directly, so care can continue before progress is lost. Referrals keep climbing — and current donor funding can't yet meet every request.

story.heading: The gap kept showing up in the treatment room.
paragraphs: 22 Strong began inside Next Level Wellness, a sports-medicine center in Newport Beach co-owned by Travis Mayfield — a Purple Heart veteran of the 101st Airborne — and Dr. Vasili “Dr. Vas” Gatsinaris.

In the treatment room they kept seeing the same problem: the care was working, but the next visit wasn't guaranteed. They built 22 Strong so high-level recovery wouldn't depend on whether someone could afford one more appointment.

veterans.text: veterans report chronic pain after service. When pain is part of daily life, recovery care should not be treated as optional.

athletes.text: U.S. children were uninsured in 2024 — the highest rate in nearly a decade — and many more are underinsured, leaving families to cover recovery care on their own.
athletes.source: Georgetown University Center for Children and Families

athletes.text: U.S. families had trouble paying their child's medical bills last year. When recovery care isn't covered, the cost falls on the family.
athletes.source: KFF / National Survey of Children's Health, 2024

others.text: is the typical cost of one outpatient physical-therapy session for someone paying without insurance.
a year is a common insurance cap on physical therapy — often less than recovery actually takes.
of parents carry health care debt — far more than non-parents — and most who do have put off needed care because of the cost.

audiences.heading: Veterans
audiences.lead: Care funding for veterans rebuilding strength, mobility, and function after service.
audiences.body: Support that respects the long work of recovery and helps keep care moving when benefits, timing, or access are not enough.

audiences.heading: Low income youth
audiences.lead: Recovery care for young people whose families can't cover what it takes to heal after an injury or setback.
audiences.body: When insurance runs out or care costs more than a family can carry, a young person's recovery can stall. 22 Strong keeps it moving — for student-athletes and other young people who need hands-on care to get back.

voices.name: Adam Folker
voices.photoAlt: Adam Folker driving to the basket for UC Irvine

luke.story: Luke shares why access to hands-on recovery care matters after service.

adam.quote: During my sophomore year in college, I had hip surgery that was potentially season-ending and even career-ending. My coaches referred me to Dr. Vas, who was able to take care of me and eventually get me back to playing within a short few weeks. That season I ended up winning hustle player of the year award. Throughout my career, I saw Dr. Vas on various occasions for injuries that came up, along with maintenance and preventive care. I was able to play competitively at the top level.

veteranVoices.name: Tom Boscamp
veteranVoices.title: U.S. Army Veteran · Veteran Services Coordinator · OCVMFC Steering Committee · NAVPA Board Member
veteranVoices.quote: I am a 65 year old Veteran who needs to be active since I am on the go taking care of Veterans in the Community. I have degenerative disk disease and Spinal Stenosis as well as radiculopathy. The VA treatments are far and few between as they do not have enough chiropractors, massage therapists, and Physical therapy specialists. Thankfully Dr. Vas has filled that void and with his treatments it has allowed me to continue being active. I have even lost 15 pounds! I can’t say enough how good Next Level Wellness is! Also their commitment to serving Veterans is “Next Level”!

veteranVoices.name: U.S. military veteran
veteranVoices.quote: Years of pain had limited what I could do. After treatment here, I got back mobility I thought was gone for good.

veteranVoices.name: U.S. military veteran
veteranVoices.quote: I came in skeptical, but the therapy worked. I have less pain now and I'm back to handling things on my own.

veteranVoices.name: U.S. military veteran
veteranVoices.quote: I could barely get around when I started. A few weeks in, I'm walking better and getting through a normal day again.

strongCircle.heading: Join the 22 Strong Circle.
strongCircle.lead: A monthly membership for people who want recovery to keep going — so 22 Strong can say yes to the next veteran or young person without waiting on the next fundraiser.
strongCircle.footnote: Monthly · cancel anytime · every dollar funds recovery care.

points.label: Continuity
points.text: Care that keeps going after the first visit.

points.label: Reliability
points.text: A steady base we can plan real recovery around.

points.label: Community
points.text: Veterans and neighbors funding recovery together.

heading: A recovery gap appears.
22 Strong helps close it.
howItWorks.kicker: The story is not complicated: someone has a real recovery plan, the next appointment becomes uncertain, and donor funding keeps the care moving.

steps.heading: Referral or request
steps.body: Referrals come from the VA, veteran networks, coaches, school counselors, and local community organizations.

steps.heading: We evaluate the gap
steps.body: 22 Strong reviews the situation. If coverage has run out, private care is out of reach, or the right connections aren’t there — we step in.

steps.heading: Care is provided
steps.body: Recovery sessions are provided directly, at no cost to the person receiving care.
steps.note: Care is currently provided through {provider} in Newport Beach — high-level sports medicine, including Fascial Integrative Treatment (FIT) for rotator cuff, knee, neck, lower back, hip, ankle/foot, and wrist/elbow injuries.

outcome.heading: Care doesn’t stop.
outcome.body: The gap closes, and recovery keeps moving — visit after visit.

leadership.name: Dr. Vasili “Dr. Vas” Gatsinaris, D.C.
paragraphs: Dr. Vas earned his Doctorate of Chiropractic from the Los Angeles College of Chiropractic in 2001 and has more than 25 years of experience helping people recover and rebuild.

Over his career, he has worked with everyone from professional athletes to first responders, developing a hands-on, integrative approach focused on restoring mobility and getting people back to life stronger and faster.
He is co-owner of Next Level Fitness & Wellness Center in Newport Beach — the same recovery care 22 Strong helps fund.

leadership.name: Travis Mayfield
paragraphs: A Purple Heart recipient who served as a Sergeant with the U.S. Army’s 101st Airborne Division in Vietnam, Travis is Co-Owner and President of Next Level Fitness & Wellness Center. His own long road back from war is the inspiration behind 22 Strong’s model. He helped found the foundation and leads its mission to make recovery care accessible to those who served and to others rebuilding their lives.
His service and community work have been recognized by the California State Senate and Assembly, the U.S. House of Representatives, and Orange County.

donate.heading: Donate today.
donate.lead: A one-time gift goes straight to the next recovery appointment someone can't otherwise reach.
donate.outcomes: In 2026, 22 Strong has funded over 200 recovery sessions for 45 people across Orange County.
donate.goalCopy: $22,000 funds around 100 recovery sessions — keeping care within reach for the people who need it most.

items.body: Help staying with care long enough for recovery to keep moving.

items.body: A path for veterans, families, and neighbors already trying to get well.
